title: Private Optimization and Statistical Physics: Low-Rank Matrix Approximation
speaker: Nisheeth Vishnoi
abstract: In this talk, I will present the following two connections between private optimization and statistical physics, both via the problem of approximating a given covariance matrix with a low-rank matrix:   An efficient algorithm to privately compute a low-rank approximation to a given matrix and how it leads to an efficient way to sample from Harish-Chandra-Itzykson-Zuber densities studied in physics and mathematics. (Based on https://arxiv.org/abs/2011.05417)

proof_stages.label: Low-rank approximation objectives and utilities
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates low-rank approximation of a covariance/data matrix, reviews the classical spectral optimum, and defines utility losses for inner-product and Frobenius-distance objectives once the exact optimum can no longer simply be released.

proof_stages.label: Differential privacy as randomized stability
proof_stages.summary: After motivating why anonymization and covariance release are unsafe, the speaker recalls epsilon/delta differential privacy as stability under changing one user and explains why private optimization must output a distribution rather than a deterministic optimizer.

proof_stages.label: Exponential mechanism on unitary orbits
proof_stages.summary: Pure differential privacy for the inner-product objective is reduced to sampling a Gibbs/log-linear density over the unitary orbit of the target spectrum, with Haar measure pushed forward to the orbit.

proof_stages.label: HCIZ orbit-sampling theorem
proof_stages.summary: The orbit-density sampling problem is identified with Harish-Chandra–Itzykson–Zuber-type densities on the unitary group, and the speaker states a polynomial-time sampling theorem for these log-linear densities on unitary orbits.

proof_stages.label: Gelfand-Tsetlin convexification of orbit sampling
proof_stages.summary: The proof idea maps a nonconvex unitary orbit by the Rayleigh map to the Gelfand-Tsetlin polytope, where the relevant measure becomes an exponential density on a convex polytope, then samples there and lifts back along the fiber.

proof_stages.label: Gaussian input perturbation mechanism and utility goal
proof_stages.summary: For approximate differential privacy, the second mechanism perturbs the input matrix by Gaussian or complex Gaussian noise and returns the spectral optimum of the perturbed matrix, aiming to improve utility beyond worst-case Davis-Kahan perturbation bounds.

proof_stages.label: Dyson Brownian motion and eigenvalue-gap control
proof_stages.summary: Viewing Gaussian perturbation as matrix Brownian motion gives Dyson SDEs for eigenvalues and eigenvectors, so the utility integral can be bounded when eigenvalue gaps do not become too small; new gap estimates and a reduction to the zero-start case address this bottleneck.

proof_stages.label: Moving orbit and complex-noise repulsion refinement
proof_stages.summary: The improved analysis lets the target orbit move with the evolving matrix and uses complex Brownian motion, whose stronger eigenvalue repulsion plus mean-field control yields the stated Frobenius-utility improvement and suggests further questions about the optimal noise geometry.

examples.name: PCA/subspace recovery special case
examples.note: The rank-k projection/PCA case, and especially k=1 as the largest-eigenvector problem, makes the general low-rank approximation formulation concrete without driving the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker specializes the general low-rank formulation to rank-k/PCA/subspace recovery, identifies the corresponding lambda choices, and describes the optimal eigenvector/truncated-spectrum solution

examples.name: Grassmannian and rank-one sphere cases
examples.note: Rank-k projection matrices form a Grassmannian, and the rank-one case reduces to a sphere/simplex picture, serving as a concrete special case of the unitary-orbit sampler.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker identifies the Grassmannian/rank-one sphere cases and later works through the rank-one case via the GT polytope simplex and an explicit fiber reconstruction using complex phases and square roots

passes.reason: Grassmannians and rank-one spheres are unitary homogeneous symmetric spaces with rank one at the minimal-rank endpoint
